findings.title: [009] RESOLVED (round-1 major): the two allocation models are now correctly distinguished; sysinfo framing is accurate
findings.description: Round-1 raised a major finding: the spec wrongly characterized the sysinfo readTar branch as an easy-to-miss CWE-789 pre-allocation sink and applied the cap with that rationale. The round-2 spec is corrected and now matches the code. Verified against source: (a) meta.* (report/report.go:170) and stat (report/report.go:203) both call NewPGresultFile(r, hdr.Size), which does make([]byte, bufsz) at internal/stat/postgres.go:519 — a buffer pre-sized from the attacker-influenceable tar header before any byte is read = the two true CWE-789 pre-allocation sinks; (b) the sysinfo.* branch (report/report.go:191) uses io.ReadAll(io.LimitReader(r, hdr.Size)), which grows incrementally by bytes actually read and so cannot over-allocate from a lying oversized header alone — not a pre-alloc sink. The spec now states exactly this in three places: Architecture 'How it works' [009] ('two true CWE-789 pre-allocation sinks' = meta+stat; sysinfo 'is not a pre-allocation sink ... grows its buffer by bytes actually read ... its purpose is consistency, not closing a pre-alloc sink'); Decision 1 ('the meta.* and stat branches (the two real pre-alloc sinks) inherit the guard via NewPGresultFile; the sysinfo.* branch adds the same cap inline as defense-in-depth'); and Risks table row 1 ('uses io.ReadAll, so it is bounded by actual bytes, not a pre-alloc CWE-789 sink'). The inaccurate 'easy-to-miss CWE-789 sink' wording is gone. The cap is still applied to all three branches (defense-in-depth on sysinfo), which is fine.
findings.location: Section: Architecture (How it works [009]); Decision 1; Risks table row 1; report/report.go:170,191,203; internal/stat/postgres.go:519
findings.impact: None remaining. The threat model is now precisely stated, so an implementer will place the make()-guard on the two real pre-alloc sinks and treat the sysinfo cap as defense-in-depth rather than as the primary mitigation.
findings.recommendation: No change required. The round-1 major is resolved. Keep the guard as the first statement in NewPGresultFile before make([]byte, bufsz), and keep the inline sysinfo cap as documented defense-in-depth.

findings.title: [009] core mitigation is sound — cap + reject before make() correctly closes CWE-789 on the two pre-allocation sinks
findings.description: Confirmed against code: NewPGresultFile (internal/stat/postgres.go:518) does data := make([]byte, bufsz) with bufsz = hdr.Size sourced directly from the tar header (report/report.go:170, :203), with no prior bound. A crafted or tampered 'pgcenter record' archive (trust boundary: report replays an on-disk archive) can declare a multi-GiB hdr.Size and force an immediate large allocation / OOM before any data is read. The proposed fix — reject when bufsz < 0 || bufsz > MaxResultFileSize before the make — is the correct sink and the correct mechanism. 256 MiB is a reasonable bound (~300x the largest observed golden entry).
findings.impact: Unbounded memory allocation / denial of service via crafted archive, before the fix.
findings.recommendation: Proceed as specified. Place the guard as the first statement in NewPGresultFile, before make([]byte, bufsz). The pinned error format (fmt.Errorf("result file size %d exceeds limit %d bytes", bufsz, MaxResultFileSize)) plus a distinct negative-size error is appropriate.

findings.title: [009] negative/zero guard is adequate — make([]byte, negative) panics, bufsz < 0 reject prevents it
findings.description: Confirmed: make([]byte, n) with n < 0 panics ('makeslice: len out of range'), which would crash pgcenter report instead of returning a clean error. The spec's explicit bufsz < 0 reject guard (Decision 1, Risks row 2) is necessary and correct. bufsz == 0 (empty entry) is correctly allowed: make([]byte, 0) is valid and io.ReadFull on a zero-length buffer returns nil. The two paths differ on negative input (meta/stat would panic at make; sysinfo's io.LimitReader with negative n reads 0 bytes without panic), which the spec now reflects by scoping the panic concern to the make() sinks. The pinned 'no int(hdr.Size) conversion in the guard' constraint keeps the comparison a pure int64 compare and avoids reintroducing a narrowing bug.
findings.impact: Without the < 0 guard, a negative tar hdr.Size crashes report with an unrecoverable panic (availability) rather than a handled error.
findings.recommendation: Keep the combined guard bufsz < 0 || bufsz > MaxResultFileSize. The table test cases (negative rejected, 0 allowed, ==limit allowed, ==limit+1 rejected) cover the boundary correctly.

findings.title: [009] int64 typing is correct and the no-G115 / no-int(hdr.Size) constraint is now explicit; no other archive-driven allocation sinks missed
findings.description: Confirmed: hdr.Size (archive/tar Header.Size) is int64, NewPGresultFile's bufsz parameter is int64, and the spec declares MaxResultFileSize as int64 (256 << 20). The comparison bufsz < 0 || bufsz > MaxResultFileSize is a pure int64 vs int64 comparison with no narrowing conversion, so gosec G115 is not triggered; the spec now explicitly forbids introducing int(hdr.Size) in the guard. Sink coverage re-checked on the tar-replay path: the only attacker-influenceable allocation sinks reachable from a replayed archive are (a) make([]byte, hdr.Size) in NewPGresultFile x2 (meta, stat) — capped; (b) io.ReadAll(io.LimitReader(r, hdr.Size)) for sysinfo — capped via header check for defense-in-depth. After allocation, json.Unmarshal into stat.PGresult / stat.SysInfo is bounded by the already-capped fixed in-memory byte slice. internal/stat/log.go make([]byte, bufsize) is the live-PostgreSQL logfile reader, not in the tar-replay path — correctly out of scope. No JSON-depth limit is applied to json.Unmarshal, but with the 256 MiB input cap and flat target structs this is a low, acceptable residual risk for this tool.
findings.location: Decision 1; internal/stat/postgres.go:518 (bufsz int64); report/report.go (hdr.Size int64); out of scope: internal/stat/log.go
findings.impact: None — typing is correct; confirms the no-G115 acceptance criterion holds and the sink inventory is complete for the tar-replay path.
findings.recommendation: Declare 'const MaxResultFileSize int64 = 256 << 20' explicitly typed; do not introduce int(hdr.Size) anywhere in the guard. Keep 'make lint' / govulncheck gates as specified. Optional (not required): note in the spec that json.Unmarshal depth is not separately bounded, accepted because the input buffer is now capped.
